[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Ramersdorf-Perlach%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Ramersdorf-Perlach%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Ramersdorf-Perlach%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Ramersdorf-Perlach%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(German pronunciation: [[ˈʁaːmɐsdɔʁf ˈpɛʁlax]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")) is a borough of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"). It is located south-east of the city center and is the most populous of Munich's boroughs with a population of about 116,000.[[1]](#cite_note-population-1) It consists of the five districts Ramersdorf, [Balanstraße](/wiki/Balanstra%C3%9Fe "Balanstraße") West, Altperlach, [Neuperlach](/wiki/Neuperlach "Neuperlach") and Waldperlach.

Borough of Munich, Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_16_%28Karte%29_-_Ramersdorf_-_Perlach.png/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_16_%28Karte%29_-_Ramersdorf_-_Perlach.png)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_16_(Karte)_-_Ramersdorf_-_Perlach.png)

District map

**Ramersdorf-Perlach** (German pronunciation: [[ˈʁaːmɐsdɔʁf ˈpɛʁlax]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")) is a borough of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"). It is located south-east of the city center and is the most populous of Munich's boroughs with a population of about 116,000.[[1]](#cite_note-population-1) It consists of the five districts Ramersdorf, [Balanstraße](/wiki/Balanstra%C3%9Fe "Balanstraße") West, Altperlach, [Neuperlach](/wiki/Neuperlach "Neuperlach") and Waldperlach.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ramersdorf-Perlach&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Karl-Preis-Platz-Munich-April-2017.jpg/250px-Karl-Preis-Platz-Munich-April-2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl-Preis-Platz-Munich-April-2017.jpg)

[Karl-Preis-Platz](/wiki/Karl-Preis-Platz_(Munich_U-Bahn) "Karl-Preis-Platz (Munich U-Bahn)") in Ramersdorf

Ramersdorf-Perlach consists of the two former municipalities *Ramersdorf* (incorporated on 1 January 1864) and *Perlach* (incorporated on 1 January 1930)[[2]](#cite_note-2) as well as the two districts *Waldperlach* and *[Neuperlach](/wiki/Neuperlach "Neuperlach")* located in the former municipal area of Perlach. The former Perlach districts of *Michaeliburg* and *Fasangarten* now largely belong to the boroughs of [Trudering-Riem](/wiki/Trudering-Riem "Trudering-Riem") and [Obergiesing-Fasangarten](/wiki/Obergiesing "Obergiesing"), respectively.

The district has eleven primary schools, nine [secondary schools](/wiki/Education_in_Germany#Secondary_education "Education in Germany") (three Mittelschulen, four Realschulen, two Gymnasiums), two special schools, one orientation school, two vocational schools, nine [Kinderkrippen](/wiki/Education_in_Germany#Preschool "Education in Germany") (children under three), 43 [Kindergärten](/wiki/Education_in_Germany#Preschool "Education in Germany") (nursery schools), 21 afternoon childcare facilities, eight recreational homes, 72 parks or green spaces (151 hectares), 44 playgrounds, four district sports facilities, two district libraries, one indoor and one outdoor swimming pool. As part of the urban area orientation of the [Münchner Volkshochschule](/wiki/Folk_high_school "Folk high school"), a branch of the Munich Adult Education Centre was set up in Neuperlach-Süd. On the district's border with Giesing, the [University of Television and Film Munich](/wiki/University_of_Television_and_Film_Munich "University of Television and Film Munich") was located until 2011. There are 75 [stops](/wiki/Railway_stop "Railway stop") of the [Münchner Verkehrsgesellschaft](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchner_Verkehrsgesellschaft "Münchner Verkehrsgesellschaft") (including five stops of the [Munich U-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_U-Bahn "Munich U-Bahn") and two of the [Munich S-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_S-Bahn "Munich S-Bahn") within the district, as well as three underground and two S-Bahn stations on the district borders, plus eleven bus or tram lines). The district committee consists of 45 members.

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ramersdorf-Perlach&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Ramersdorf-Perlach is home to one of Munich's oldest churches, the [St. Mary's Church](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Church,_Ramersdorf "St. Mary's Church, Ramersdorf") in Ramersdorf which was mentioned for the first time in 1315. Two further old churches in the settlement of Perlach are the St. Michael's church and the St. Paul's church which is the oldest Protestant church in Munich.

### Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ramersdorf-Perlach&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Economy")]

Several large companies such as for example [Allianz](/wiki/Allianz "Allianz"), [Siemens AG](/wiki/Siemens_AG "Siemens AG"), [Wacker Chemie](/wiki/Wacker_Chemie "Wacker Chemie") or [BSH Home Appliances](/wiki/BSH_Hausger%C3%A4te "BSH Hausgeräte") have headquarters or operate development centers in the district of Neuperlach. The editorial office of the teenage magazine [Bravo](/wiki/Bravo_(magazine) "Bravo (magazine)") is also based in Neuperlach, as well as one of Munich's largest shopping malls, the [pep](/wiki/Einkaufs-Center_Neuperlach_%E2%80%93_pep "Einkaufs-Center Neuperlach – pep").

### Recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ramersdorf-Perlach&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Recreation")]

The public park [Ostpark](/wiki/Ostpark_(Munich) "Ostpark (Munich)"), Munich's largest waterpark [Michaelibad](/w/index.php?title=Michaelibad&action=edit&redlink=1 "Michaelibad (page does not exist)") and the cemetery [Neuer Südfriedhof](/w/index.php?title=Neuer_S%C3%BCdfriedhof&action=edit&redlink=1 "Neuer Südfriedhof (page does not exist)") are located in Ramersdorf-Perlach. The small river [Hachinger Bach](/wiki/Hachinger_Bach "Hachinger Bach") enters Munich in Perlach.

### Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ramersdorf-Perlach&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Culture")]

The museum of the Munich public transport company [MVG](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchner_Verkehrsgesellschaft "Münchner Verkehrsgesellschaft") ist located at [Ständlerstraße](/wiki/St%C3%A4ndlerstra%C3%9Fe "Ständlerstraße") in the Balanstraße West district.

## Ramersdorf

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ramersdorf-Perlach&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ramersdorf")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/M%C3%BCnchen_und_Umgebung_1856.jpg/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_und_Umgebung_1856.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_und_Umgebung_1856.jpg)

Ramersdorf and Perlach on a map from 1858

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Ramersdorf_postkarte.jpg/250px-Ramersdorf_postkarte.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ramersdorf_postkarte.jpg)

[St. Mary's Church](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Church,_Ramersdorf "St. Mary's Church, Ramersdorf")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Ramersdorf-Mittlerer-Ring.jpg/250px-Ramersdorf-Mittlerer-Ring.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ramersdorf-Mittlerer-Ring.jpg)

Ramersdorf with Middle Ring

*Rumoltesdorf* was first mentioned in documents between 1006 and 1022 in a [Freising](/wiki/Freising "Freising") document transcript. However, the suffix *-dorf* suggests that a settlement may have already existed in the second half of the 9th century, especially since individual members of the *Rumoltes* family have been attested in documents since the 8th century. From the 13th to the 16th century, various ministerial dynasties and patrician families are mentioned as owners of the farms; some of these properties were also given to the church as endowments until they passed back into secular ownership during the [Secularisation in Bavaria](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization") in 1803. From 1818 until its incorporation into Munich in 1864, Ramersdorf was a separate municipality. A far-reaching transformation of the district took place during the rule of [National Socialism](/wiki/National_Socialism "National Socialism") through the large-scale road construction and with the Mustersiedlung Ramersdorf (model housing estate for [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany")).

The [St. Mary's Church](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Church,_Ramersdorf "St. Mary's Church, Ramersdorf") marks the old centre of the village, which is now rather squeezed in between the [Middle Ring](/wiki/Bundesstra%C3%9Fe_2_R "Bundesstraße 2 R") and the access road to the [Munich-Salzburg](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_8 "Bundesautobahn 8") motorway. The Ramersdorf church has been documented since the beginning of the 11th century and, along with [Altötting](/wiki/Alt%C3%B6tting "Altötting"), is probably one of the oldest pilgrimage sites in [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"). Pilgrimages to the image of the Virgin Mary probably began at the beginning of the 14th century, and in this century a *particle of the cross* also came into the possession of the church. In the 15th and 16th centuries the church was rebuilt and in the 17th century extensive [Baroqueisations](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") were carried out. The present tower dates from the end of the 18th century. At the beginning of the 19th century, many [votive](/wiki/Votive "Votive") panels and other representations that paid homage to an exuberant cult of saints were demolished "in the name of the Enlightenment". After the foundation of a parish of its own in 1907 (*Maria Ramersdorf* had until then been a daughter church of St. Michael Perlach), extensive restorations were undertaken, which, however, at the same time led to the discovery of older components of the church, for example from the [Gothic period](/wiki/Gothic_period "Gothic period").

Appreciation: "Both the records of the building and pilgrimage history as well as the high-quality furnishings, including the precious cross particle and the Ramersdorf image of grace, and not least the late Gothic carved altar attributed to [Erasmus Grasser](/wiki/Erasmus_Grasser "Erasmus Grasser"), attest to the high artistic and cultural-historical value of the Ramersdorf church." (Karin Hösch)

Based on the documents, several district initiatives and organisations decided in 2005 to celebrate Ramersdorf's thousandth anniversary in 2006.
